event_summary: The MV Hondius arrived in Rotterdam on 18 May 2026 carrying at least twelve confirmed hantavirus cases; the WHO assessed global risk as low, Canada confirmed its first case, and Australia quarantined returning passengers for three weeks.
tldr: Headline framing is overwhelmingly HEALTH_SAFETY across 24 of 26 buckets — case counts, quarantine logistics, and the WHO 'low risk' verdict dominate at the title layer before any body text is read. A POLICY_PRESCRIPTION subset (Japan-UK antiviral supply, French surveillance, Australian quarantine rules) is visible in four buckets, and Turkey plus The Conversation carry a thin PUBLIC_OPINION thread equating the outbreak with COVID-era pandemic anxiety. Spain's sole contribution — a headline reading only 'Crónica' — is the clearest headline-layer silence in the corpus. India's Hindustan Times diverges sharply with a domestic US crime headline tangential to hantavirus, the only title in the corpus that fits neither health nor policy frames.

isolation_top.note: Single headline 'Crónica' (a bare section label) contains no story-specific semantic content — LaBSE embedding is effectively noise, producing the lowest similarity in the corpus.
Italian headline focuses on Rotterdam port logistics ('Rotterdam si prepara all'arrivo...') — a procedural angle that diverges from the case-count and quarantine dominant.
Finnish press digest buries hantavirus in a multi-story headline ('Quarantined Finns, encouraging economic signs, and TVs go dark'), diluting its alignment with the corpus.
Korea Herald frames the outbreak as a spotlight moment for South Korea's founding role in hantavirus discovery — a national-pride angle absent from all other buckets.
Japan Times headline focuses on antiviral drug diplomacy (Japan-UK supply) rather than the outbreak itself, creating semantic distance from the case-count majority.

silences.what_they_covered_instead: The sole available headline is 'Crónica' — a genre section label that provides no identifiable framing of the MV Hondius outbreak or hantavirus at the title level.

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only headline in the corpus to frame hantavirus through a domestic US crime story — an Arkansas man threatening a Walmart shooting over lockdown fears — the one title that fits neither HEALTH_SAFETY nor POLICY_PRESCRIPTION and signals how hantavirus fear is diffusing into unrelated domestic narratives in some outlets.

bottom_line: Headline coverage of the Hondius outbreak is locked in a tight HEALTH_SAFETY frame — case counts, quarantine logistics, and the WHO 'low global risk' verdict dominate 24 of 26 buckets before any body text is read. The headline layer systematically suppresses the governance and accountability angles (CDC notification failures, tourist-economy damage) that appear in body text, making this story a strong candidate for headline-body divergence in the sensationalism index.
